turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: In case number 17-2, United States v. Microsoft, we are issuing a per curiam opinion. In 2013 federal law enforcement agents served a warrant on Microsoft requiring it to disclose all emails associated with one of its accounts to the extent the information was within Microsoft's possession, custody, or control. Microsoft determined that the pertinent emails were stored in a single location, Microsoft's data center in Dublin, Ireland. The company declined to produce those emails, arguing that requiring it to do so would be an impermissible extraterritorial application of US law. The District Court disagreed and found Microsoft in contempt, but the Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit reversed, ruling in favor of Microsoft. We granted the government's petition for review and heard argument in the case on February 27 of this year. On March 23, Congress enacted and the President signed into law the Clarifying Lawful Overseas Use of Data Act. The acronym works out to be the CLOUD Act. That Act includes a provision specifying that, and this is a quote, "a service provider shall comply with the obligations of this chapter to preserve, backup, or disclose the contents of a wire or electronic communication and any record or other information pertaining to a customer or subscriber within such provider's possession, custody, or control, regardless of whether such communication, record, or other information is located within or outside the United States." Now, the government promptly went to court and obtained pursuant to the new law a new warrant covering the information requested in the warrant at issue in this case. The parties agreed that the new warrant has replaced the original warrant. So this case about a superseded warrant under a superseded law has become moot. Following the Court's established practice in such cases, the judgment on review is vacated and the case is remanded to the Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit with instructions, first, to vacate the District Court's orders and then to direct the District Court to dismiss the case as moot. Our opinion is unanimous.
